science.title: Researchers examine graphene wetting transparency from macroscopic window to nanoscale mirror
science.description: Researchers at the Max Planck Institute for Polymer Research, University of Cambridge, Institute for Basic Science (IBS), Korea University and Durham University have uncovered how graphene can appear “wetting transparent” at large scales while still reshaping water structure at the nanoscale. Graphene on a substrate and in contact with water underpins technologies from desalination and energy storage to sensing, electrocatalysis, and neuromorphic devices, yet its wettability has remained controversial. Suspended graphene on water is intrinsically hydrophobic, but when graphene is supported on oxides or ionic crystals, macroscopic contact‑angle measurements show that the apparent wettability is largely dictated by the underlying substrate - an effect known as wetting transparency. The new work asks the question: do substrate electric fields simply pass through graphene, or does graphene’s pronounced polarizability first reshape those fields and thereby mediate how water is organized at the interface? To answer this, the team combined heterodyne‑detected sum‑frequency generation (HD‑SFG) spectroscopy with molecular dynamics simulations on calcium fluoride (CaF₂) crystals, studied either bare or covered by a single graphene layer in contact with water. By tuning the solution pH, they adjusted the surface charge of CaF₂ and directly tracked how interfacial water molecules orient and hydrogen‑bond, both with and without graphene. The HD‑SFG spectra show that, when averaged over micrometer‑scale probing areas, the water structures at the two interfaces are very similar and both vary strongly with pH, demonstrating that substrate electrostatics and proton‑mediated surface chemistry govern the interfacial water arrangement and provide the molecular basis of graphene’s apparent wetting transparency.

science.title: Scientists may have discovered ancient ocean lines on Mars
science.description: Like Earth and the solar system's other planets, Mars formed roughly 4.5 billion years ago. Early in its history, Mars was warmer and wetter than the cold and arid place it is today.

science.title: Exploring the Invisible: The Vacuum's Compelling Secrets
science.description: The concept of the vacuum, initially thought to be empty, is actually steeped in complex physics. From Aristotle's horror vacui to quantum field fluctuations, this piece explores the transition from ancient philosophical ideas to groundbreaking discoveries, like Torricelli's void and the quantum vacuum's measurable properties.

Limited pure-play AI opportunities in India are pushing ultra-HNIs to allocate more capital to global markets, especially the US, where AI, cloud, and digital infrastructure offer broader opportunities, aided by easier overseas investing routes like GIFT City.
